accent_and_dynamics.description: Hit harder, often on beats 1 and 3.
Muted or very soft strum for texture.
Rest palm lightly on strings near bridge for a percussive, dampened sound.
Release fretting hand pressure after strumming for a percussive 'chick' sound (reggae / ska).

common_styles.description: Fast, rolling strum using multiple fingers (down-up-down-up in rapid succession).
Plucking strings individually with fingers (p, i, m, a) rather than strumming.
D - U - D - U with accent on off-beats (2 and 4), often using chuck (muted strum).
D D U U D U, steady and full, good for singing along.

common_styles.example_4_4: p - i - m - i  (thumb on bass, then index, middle, index on treble strings)

tips: Use a light touch for upstrokes, stronger for downstrokes.
Keep your strumming hand moving constantly (down-up motion) even on rests.
For vihuela: use lighter, faster strokes; gut/nylon strings respond to nail or fingertip.
For ukulele: strum over the neck joint (not soundhole) for brighter tone.
For guitar: vary strumming position (near bridge = brighter, over soundhole = fuller).
